L1 Bienvenue
Badges & rewards 0 / 0

Every badge unlocks a surprise reward — collect from Daniel in class. The Top 3 in Hi-Score earn something extra at the last class. The bigger the badge, the bigger the surprise.

Welcome to Fluolingo Pretest

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris. Wei arrives in Paris and needs survival French for the boulangerie, the classroom, and first meetings with Amara, Mateo, and Yuki.

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group. Wei, Amara, Mateo, and Yuki learn to introduce themselves and describe nationalities, professions, ages, countries, and languages.

Unit 2 · Daily life above the boulangerie

Épisode 2 — Une famille au-dessus de la boulangerie. The four discover everyday routines in the apartment above Madame Benali’s boulangerie: places, objects, invitations, time, notes, and messages.

Unit 3 · Paris, weather, and directions

Épisode 3 — Je suis perdue. Rain, streets, métro routes, questions, attractions, and directions help the group find its way through Paris.

Unit 4 · Le grand dîner and Bilan

Épisode 4 — Le grand dîner; Épisode 5 — Bilan. Amara proposes a dinner. The group plans, shops, cooks, gives advice, discusses quantities, and reflects after the meal.

LAF1201

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

Greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous — everything Wei needs on arrival in Paris.

LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerS’appeler — tilesGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Tu vs vous

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Pick tu or vous across 11 social situations. Tests whether you can read relationship + setting rather than just age — strangers and service roles take vous, family and animals take tu, and any plural addressee takes vous regardless of closeness.

1. You meet your French professor for the first time.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

2. You meet a classmate your age in LAF1201.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

3. An elderly woman you don't know asks you for directions.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

4. You're talking to your own grandfather at a family meal.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

5. You speak to a 7-year-old child at a family dinner.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

6. You're at your friend's home for dinner. You have never met their dad before — he opens the door.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

7. You're texting two close friends together in a group chat — you address them both at once.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

8. You order a coffee from a barista you don't know.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

9. You're playing fetch with your friend's dog.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

10. A 10-year-old child speaks to a 70-year-old man they don't know.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

11. The older man addresses the child.
Which pronoun do you use — tu or vous?

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerS’appeler — tilesGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Subject pronouns

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Match the right subject pronoun (je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles) to who is being talked about. Drills the person + number grid before any verb conjugation — the foundation every later exercise stands on.

1. Talking about yourself.

2. Talking to one friend, classmate, or family member.

3. Talking about one person (or thing of the masculine gender).

4. Talking about one person (or thing of the feminine gender).

5. Talking about people in general (e.g. saying one is/does something), or casually saying "we".

6. Talking about yourself and at least one other person.

7. Talking to one person with a respectful (non-intimate) distance — a teacher, stranger, elder.

8. Talking to two or more people — same word, plural meaning.

9. Talking about a group of men, OR a mixed group.

10. Talking about a group of women only.

11. ___ est colombien. He is Colombian. (talking about Mateo)

12. ___ sont dans la classe. They are in class. (Wei and Yuki)

13. ___ êtes boulangère, madame ? Are you a baker, ma'am? (formal)

14. ___ mange ensemble ce soir ? Shall we eat together tonight? (casual)

15. ___ parles français ? Do you speak French? (to a classmate)

16. ___ sommes étudiants à Paris. We are students in Paris.

17. ___ est japonaise. She is Japanese. (talking about Yuki)

18. ___ habitent dans le même quartier. They live in the same neighbourhood. (Mateo and Wei)

19. ___ m'appelle Amara. My name is Amara. (I call myself Amara)

20. ___ comprenez la question ? Do you understand the question? (teacher to class)

21. Le café ? ___ est fermé aujourd'hui. The café? It's closed today.

22. La boulangerie ? ___ ouvre à sept heures. The bakery? It opens at seven.

23. En France, ___ mange du pain tous les jours. In France, people eat bread every day.

24. ___ habites à Paris aussi ? Do you live in Paris too? (Wei to Mateo)

25. Les étudiants ? ___ arrivent à neuf heures. The students? They arrive at nine.

26. ___ viens du Sénégal. I come from Senegal.

27. ___ étudions le français ensemble. We study French together.

28. Madame Sow ? ___ est professeure. Madame Sow? She is a teacher.

29. ___ aime le chocolat chaud. He likes hot chocolate. (talking about Mateo)

30. ___ parlent ensemble après le cours. They talk together after class. (Amara and Yuki)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

Focus

Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerS’appeler — tilesGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Stress pronouns

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Choose the right stress pronoun (moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles) after prepositions, alone, or for emphasis. The set looks similar to subject pronouns but behaves differently — this drill isolates where stress forms appear. Mouse-over or tap the French sentence in the question to see its translation.

1. Mateo : Qui est là ? Wei : C'est ___ ! Mateo: Who's there? Wei: It's ___ !

2. Wei : Moi, c'est Wei. Et ___ ? Wei: Me, I'm Wei. And ___ ?

3. Amara : C'est Mateo. Je suis avec ___ en classe. Amara: That's Mateo. I'm with ___ in class.

4. Wei : Yuki est japonaise. Je parle avec ___ en français. Wei: Yuki is Japanese. I speak with ___ in French.

5. Wei et Mateo entrent. Benali : Ces croissants sont pour ___ ? Wei and Mateo walk in. Benali: These croissants are for ___ (us)?

6. Benali [aux quatre étudiants] : Ces croissants sont pour ___ ! Benali [to the four students]: These croissants are for ___ (you)!

7. Yuki : Wei et Mateo sont sympas. J'étudie avec ___. Yuki: Wei and Mateo are nice. I study with ___.

8. Mateo : Amara et Yuki sont dans mon groupe. J'étudie avec ___. Mateo: Amara and Yuki are in my group. I study with ___.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerS’appeler — tilesGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Stress vs subject

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Subject pronouns go before the verb. Stress pronouns go after prepositions, alone, or for emphasis.
Focus — Forced contrast: same person, two forms — pick subject (before the verb) or stress (after a preposition, alone, or emphatic). Targets the je/moi, tu/toi, il/lui, ils/eux pairs where confusion is most likely.

1. ___ suis étudiant. I am a student.

2. ___, je suis français. Me, I’m French. (emphasis)

3. ___ es d’où ? Where are you from?

4. Et ___, tu es d’où ? And you, where are you from?

5. ___ parle français. He speaks French.

6. ___, il est mexicain. Him, he’s Mexican. (emphasis)

7. ___ sont sympas. They are nice.

8. ___, ils sont dans mon groupe. Them, they’re in my group. (emphasis)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Rule
Subject pronoun = before the verb (je, tu, il, elle, on, nous, vous, ils, elles)
Rule
Stress pronoun = after preposition, alone, or for emphasis (moi, toi, lui, elle, nous, vous, eux, elles)
Tip
For elle, nous, vous — the forms are identical. For il/lui, je/moi, tu/toi, ils/eux — they differ.
LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Unit 0The alphabet

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Decode French letter names. Distractors target the high-confusion pairs — G/J (both soft sounds), I/Y ("i" vs "i grec"), B/V, E/I, and P/T/D — exactly the contrasts French dictation traps you with.

1. Which letter do you hear?

2. Which letter do you hear?

3. Which letter do you hear?

4. Which letter do you hear?

5. Which letter do you hear?

6. Which letter do you hear?

7. Which letter do you hear?

8. Which letter do you hear?

9. Which letter do you hear?

10. Which letter do you hear?

11. Which letter do you hear?

12. Which letter do you hear?

13. Which letter do you hear?

14. Which letter do you hear?

15. Which letter do you hear?

16. Which letter do you hear?

17. Which letter do you hear?

18. Which letter do you hear?

19. Which letter do you hear?

20. Which letter do you hear?

21. Which letter do you hear?

22. Which letter do you hear?

23. Which letter do you hear?

24. Which letter do you hear?

25. Which letter do you hear?

26. Which letter do you hear?

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerS’appeler — tilesGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Question words

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Pick the right interrogative — qui, que, quoi, où, quand, pourquoi, comment, combien — for the answer being requested. Trains question word ↔ answer type matching: a person needs qui, a place needs où, a quantity needs combien.

1. C'est ___ ? — À Paris. Where is it? — In Paris.

2. ___ tu t'appelles ? — Je m'appelle Wei. What's your name? — My name is Wei.

3. ___ commence le cours ? — À neuf heures. When does class start? — At nine o'clock.

4. ___ est ce monsieur ? — C'est Mateo. Who is this man? — It's Mateo.

5. Ça coûte ___ ? — Deux euros. How much does it cost? — Two euros.

6. Tu fais ___ ? — J'étudie le français. What are you doing? — I'm studying French.

7. ___ ça va ? — Ça va bien, merci. How are you? — I'm fine, thanks.

8. ___ est la boulangerie ? — En face du métro. Where is the bakery? — Opposite the metro.

9. On part ___ ? — Demain matin. When do we leave? — Tomorrow morning.

10. ___ parle ? — C'est Amara. Who's speaking? — It's Amara.

11. Tu as ___ de frères ? — Trois. How many brothers do you have? — Three.

12. C'est ___ ? — C'est un croissant. What is it? — It's a croissant.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Unit 0Numbers

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Two parts: (1) 5 random multiple-choice questions on numbers 0–20 (different 5 each visit), and (2) 10 audio-dictation questions where you press Play to hear a French number, then type the word as you hear it. Hyphens and accents both matter — dix-sept, zéro.

1. What is 0 in French?

2. What is 1 in French?

3. What is 2 in French?

4. What is 3 in French?

5. What is 4 in French?

6. What is 5 in French?

7. What is 6 in French?

8. What is 7 in French?

9. What is 8 in French?

10. What is 9 in French?

11. What is 10 in French?

12. What is 11 in French?

13. What is 12 in French?

14. What is 13 in French?

15. What is 14 in French?

16. What is 15 in French?

17. What is 16 in French?

18. What is 17 in French?

19. What is 18 in French?

20. What is 19 in French?

21. What is 20 in French?

22. Listen and write the number in French.

23. Listen and write the number in French.

24. Listen and write the number in French.

25. Listen and write the number in French.

26. Listen and write the number in French.

27. Listen and write the number in French.

28. Listen and write the number in French.

29. Listen and write the number in French.

30. Listen and write the number in French.

31. Listen and write the number in French.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Unit 0Days

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Name the day, identify its position in the week, and produce lundi → dimanche. Two written-form pitfalls drilled here: days are lowercase in French, and the week starts on Monday, not Sunday.

1. Which French word means Monday?

2. Which French word means Tuesday?

3. Which French word means Wednesday?

4. Which French word means Thursday?

5. Which French word means Friday?

6. Which French word means Saturday?

7. Which French word means Sunday?

8. Which French word covers every hour before noon — including 1 AM, 3 AM, and 11 AM?

9. Which French word means afternoon (noon to ~6pm)?

10. Which French word means evening / tonight (~6pm onwards — includes late evening)?

11. Which French word means the night (as a period) (the sleeping hours / late-night hours)?

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus

Focus

Unit 0Colours

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Recognise basic colour terms and start noticing gender agreement on the noun: un sac vert vs une voiture verte. First exposure to the rule that adjectives change shape — colour is the safest place to meet it.

1. Which French word means red? une voiture rouge

2. Which French word means blue? le ciel bleu

3. Which French word means green? un thé vert

4. Which French word means yellow? le soleil jaune

5. Which French word means black? un café noir

6. Which French word means white? du vin blanc

7. Which French word means orange? un fruit orange

8. Which French word means pink? une fleur rose

9. Which French word means brown? un sac marron

10. Which French word means purple? un raisin violet

11. Which French word means grey? un nuage gris

12. Which French word means beige? un mur beige

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0S’appeler

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Conjugate the reflexive verb s'appeler across all six persons (je m'appelle, tu t'appelles…). Drills two things at once: the reflexive pronoun in front of the verb, and the double-l in j'appelle / tu t'appelles / ils s'appellent vs nous nous appelons.

1. [Moi,] Je ___ Dan. My name is Dan. (literally: I call myself Dan.)

2. [Toi,] Tu ___ Marie ? Is your name Marie? (literally: Do you call yourself Marie?)

3. [Lui,] Il ___ Pierre. His name is Pierre. (literally: He calls himself Pierre.)

4. [Elle,] Elle ___ Juliette. Her name is Juliette.

5. [Nous,] Nous ___ Marc et Léa. Our names are Marc and Léa. (literally: We call ourselves Marc and Léa.)

6. [Vous,] Vous ___ comment ? What is your name? (formal/plural)

7. [Eux,] Ils ___ Pierre et Marc. Their names are Pierre and Marc.

8. [Elles,] Elles ___ Marie et Léa. Their names are Marie and Léa. (all female)

9. [Moi,] Bonjour, je ___ Claude. Hello, my name is Claude.

10. [Toi,] Comment tu ___ ? What's your name? (informal)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 · Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4

Unit 0S'appeler — word order

Focus — Place the reflexive pronoun correctly. Tap tiles to build each sentence.

1. Put in order: My name is Wei.

2. Put in order: What's your name? (informal)

3. Put in order: His name is Mateo.

4. Put in order: Her name is Yuki.

5. Put in order: We call ourselves the four.

6. Put in order: What are your names? (plural/formal)

7. Put in order: Their names are Wei and Yuki.

8. Put in order: What's your name? (informal, question-word first)

9. Put in order: My name is not Pierre.

10. Put in order: Her name is not Marie.

11. Put in order: What is your name? (formal)

12. Put in order: I introduce myself.

13. Put in order: He gets up early.

14. Put in order: We wake up at seven o'clock.

15. Put in order: You go to bed late.

16. Put in order: They walk around Paris. (fem.)

LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Greetings

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Match the right greeting to the moment: arrival vs departure, time of day, formal vs informal. Tests register fit — salut with classmates, bonjour with strangers, bonsoir from late afternoon, au revoir when you leave.

1. It's 9am. You meet your female French professor in the hallway for the first time.

2. You see your classmate just before class starts.

3. It's 8pm. You greet a stranger you're seated next to at a dinner.

4. You greet your closest friend as you arrive at a casual gathering.

5. At a professional event, you turn to the man beside you and ask him his name. What do you say?

6. You're chatting with a fellow student your age and want to know the name of this student.

7. Someone at a dinner party says « Je m'appelle Sophie. » My name is Sophie. What do you reply?

8. It's your first day of class. The professor asks you to introduce yourself. What do you say?

9. Class is over. You say goodbye specifically to your professor as you leave.

10. You're leaving a friend's place after a short visit. Which phrase is NOT appropriate here?

11. Class ends on Tuesday. You'll see your classmate again tomorrow. What do you say as you leave?

12. You wave goodbye to a shopkeeper as you leave the store, around 7pm.

13. Class ends in the early afternoon. You leave the classroom and want to wish the professor a good rest of the day.

14. You're heading to bed and say this to family before sleeping.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 0 · Survival French

Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
Tu vs vousSubject pronounsStress pronounsStress vs subjectThe alphabetQuestion wordsNumbersDaysColoursS’appelerS’appeler — tilesGreetingsSurvival French capstone

Unit 0Survival French capstone

Opening Paris scenes: Madame Benali’s boulangerie, the first classroom meeting, and the first names exchanged among the four learners.
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus — Your first day as a newcomer in Paris, run end-to-end. Names land on your ear and you spell them back; introductions happen and you pick tu or vous; you ask someone what they're called. The questions follow the arc of a first day — hearing → reading the room → asking back — so what you're being tested on is not the rules in isolation but whether they fire in the order real encounters demand them.

1. You hear the name spelled aloud: . Which name is it?

2. You hear the name spelled aloud: . Which name is it?

3. You meet your French professor for the first time at the start of class.

4. Assemble the formal self-introduction in the correct order.

5. You hear the name spelled aloud: . Which name is it?

6. You're at a friend's birthday party. A stranger your age introduces themselves to your friend (the birthday boy), then turns to you. What do you say back?

7. Assemble the casual self-introduction in the correct order.

8. You hear the name spelled aloud: . Which name is it?

9. You're chatting with a fellow student in your class. You want to know the name of this student.

10. You want to ask your female French professor what her name is.

11. ___ vous appelez comment ? What is your name? (formal)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Focus
Épisode 1 — Bonjour, Paris
Focus
Focus on recognition and first production: greetings, names, spelling, numbers, days, colours, questions, and tu/vous.
Focus

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Introductions

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Stitch together name + nationality + age + profession into a single self-presentation. Tests verb choice across the introduction script: être for who/what you are, avoir for age, s'appeler for name.

1. You meet a fellow LAF1201 student in the corridor before class.

2. You meet your French professor for the first time at the start of class.

3. At a campus social event. Someone has just introduced themselves to you.

4. You're at a Tuesday-night French conversation meet-up. Someone sits next to you.

5. You're registering for a CLS formal workshop. The receptionist needs your name.

6. You're introducing your friend Marc to your professor.

7. At a café, you're introducing your friend Léa to another friend, Jules.

8. A friend asks who that woman in the photo is.

9. You point out a famous Senegalese actor in a film poster.

10. A photo of two French chefs you admire — Anne-Sophie Pic and Hélène Darroze.

11. A photo of two French actors — Omar Sy and Marion Cotillard.

12. You've just told a new acquaintance your name. You want to keep the conversation going.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus

LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Épisodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4

Unit 1Introductions — word order

Focus — Build complete introduction sentences. Tap tiles in the correct order.

1. Put in order: Hello, my name is Wei.

2. Put in order: I am Singaporean. (fem.)

3. Put in order: I am twenty-one years old.

4. Put in order: I am a French student. (fem.)

5. Put in order: I come from Singapore.

6. Put in order: I speak English and Mandarin.

7. Put in order: Nice to meet you, my name is Mateo.

8. Put in order: I am Mexican.

9. Put in order: I live in Paris.

10. Put in order: I am a French teacher.

11. Put in order: I study French in Paris.

12. Put in order: She is twenty-two years old.

13. Put in order: Hi, I'm Amara.

14. Put in order: We are students in Paris.

15. Put in order: His name is Mateo and he is Mexican.

16. Put in order: She is Japanese and she lives in Paris.

LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Subject pronoun frames

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Slot the right subject pronoun into full sentence frames already containing a conjugated verb. The reverse of 0-02 — there you saw the person and chose the pronoun, here you see the verb form and infer who must be the subject.

1. You are introducing yourself to a new classmate. ___ m'appelle Wei. My name is Wei.

2. You are asking your classmate Marie what her name is. Comment ___ t'appelles ? What's your name? (informal)

3. You are asking your French professor what their name is on day one. Madame, ___ vous appelez comment ? Madame, what's your name?

4. You are pointing to your friend Marc in a photo. ___ s'appelle Marc. His name is Marc.

5. You are introducing your sister Léa to a friend. ___ s'appelle Léa. Her name is Léa.

6. You and your roommate are introducing yourselves to a landlord. ___ sommes étudiants. We are students.

7. You and your friends, hanging out casually. Talking about going to a café together. ___ va au café. We're going to the café. (casual)

8. You are addressing two friends together — they're both your peers. ___ êtes étudiants ? Are you students?

9. You are describing two friends — one male (Pierre) and one female (Léa). ___ sont étudiants. They are students.

10. You are describing two friends — Marie and Léa, both female. ___ sont étudiantes. They are students. (all female)

11. A French teacher addressing one university student during office hours. Bonjour, ___ êtes Wei Tan ? Hello, are you Wei Tan?

12. A waiter speaking to a group of friends sitting at a café table. Bonjour, ___ désirez quoi ? Hello, what would you like?

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Être and identity

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Conjugate être across all six persons in identity statements (nationality, profession, role). The forms suis, es, est, sommes, êtes, sont are all irregular — pure memorisation, with the highest stakes since être anchors most identity sentences.

1. Saying your nationality Je ___ singapourien. I am Singaporean (male).

2. Asking a peer their nationality Tu ___ français ? Are you French? (informal)

3. Stating someone's profession Il ___ professeur. He is a professor.

4. Stating nationality (feminine) Elle ___ française. She is French.

5. Casual "we" with friends On ___ étudiants à NUS. We are students at NUS. (casual)

6. Stating "we" formally — multiple Singaporeans Nous ___ singapouriens. We are Singaporean.

7. Asking formally Vous ___ le professeur de français ? Are you the French professor?

8. Plural identity (mixed group OR all male) Ils ___ journalistes. They are journalists.

9. All-female plural Marie et Léa ___ étudiantes en littérature. Marie and Léa are literature students.

10. Self-introduction in a professional context Bonjour, je ___ le nouveau prof. Hello, I'm the new professor.

11. Asking origin (peer) Tu ___ de Singapour ? Are you from Singapore? (informal)

12. Asking formally about nationality Madame, vous ___ française ou belge ? Madame, are you French or Belgian?

13. Wei ___ singapourienne. Wei is Singaporean (female).

14. Nous ___ dans la même classe. We are in the same class.

15. Tu ___ colombien, Mateo ? Are you Colombian, Mateo?

16. Amara et Mateo ___ étudiants. Amara and Mateo are students.

17. Vous ___ professeure, madame ? Are you a teacher, ma'am?

18. Je ___ sénégalaise. I am Senegalese (female). — Amara speaking

19. Mateo ___ content aujourd'hui. Mateo is happy today.

20. Wei et Yuki ___ japonaise et singapourienne. Wei and Yuki are Japanese and Singaporean.

21. Tu ___ fatiguée, Amara ? Are you tired, Amara?

22. Madame Benali ___ algérienne. Madame Benali is Algerian.

23. Je ___ en retard, pardon ! I am late, sorry!

24. Vous ___ prêts pour l'examen ? Are you ready for the exam? (teacher to class)

25. Mateo ___ musicien. Mateo is a musician.

26. Nous ___ contents d'être à Paris. We are happy to be in Paris.

27. Yuki ___ timide mais gentille. Yuki is shy but kind.

28. Tu ___ d'accord, Amara ? Do you agree, Amara?

29. Les quatre amis ___ internationaux. The four friends are international.

30. Madame Sow ___ patiente avec les étudiants. Madame Sow is patient with the students.

31. Je ___ informaticienne. I am a computer scientist (female). — Wei speaking

32. Vous ___ d'Algérie, Madame Benali ? Are you from Algeria, Madame Benali?

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Épisodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4

Unit 1Être / avoir — word order

Focus — Build sentences with être and avoir. Tap tiles in the correct order.

1. Put in order: I am a student.

2. Put in order: She is French.

3. Put in order: We are in Paris.

4. Put in order: They are teachers.

5. Put in order: You are Singaporean.

6. Put in order: You are students. (fem.)

7. Put in order: He is Mexican.

8. Put in order: They are Japanese. (fem.)

9. Put in order: We are happy.

10. Put in order: I am tired today.

11. Put in order: He/She is a good teacher.

12. Put in order: These are my friends.

13. Put in order: He is twenty years old.

14. Put in order: We have a cat.

15. Put in order: She has blue eyes.

16. Put in order: I have two brothers.

LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Professions

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Pick the profession term and apply gender + no article: French says Je suis professeur, not un professeur, after être. Also drills the m/f endings — étudiant / étudiante, serveur / serveuse, infirmier / infirmière.

1. Elle est ___ . She is a journalist.

2. Elle est ___ . She is a student.

3. Il est ___ . He is a student.

4. Elle est ___ . She is an actress.

5. Il est ___ . He is an actor.

6. Elle est ___ . She is a baker.

7. Elle est ___ . She is a musician.

8. Il est ___ . He is a photographer.

9. Elle est ___ . She is a chef.

10. Il est ___ . He is a fashion designer.

11. Elle est ___ . She is a lawyer.

12. Elle est ___ . She is a cook.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Countries

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Identify country names and their grammatical gender — la France, le Canada, les États-Unis. Sets up the next exercise: nationalities derive from country names, and the gender you pick here decides what the adjective looks like there.

1. Which article goes before Japon? (masculine, consonant start)

2. Which article goes before France? (feminine, ends in -e)

3. Which article goes before Italie? (vowel-starting feminine)

4. Which article goes before États-Unis? (plural country name)

5. Which article goes before Chine? (feminine, ends in -e)

6. Which article goes before Mexique? (masculine despite -e ending)

7. Which article goes before Allemagne? (vowel-starting feminine)

8. Which article goes before Canada? (masculine, ends in -a)

9. Which article goes before Pays-Bas? (plural country name)

10. Which article goes before Corée du Sud? (feminine, ends in -e)

11. Which article goes before Iran? (vowel-starting masculine)

12. Which article goes before Brésil? (masculine, consonant start)

13. Which article goes before Argentine? (feminine, vowel start)

14. Which article goes before Espagne? (feminine, vowel start)

15. Which article goes before Angleterre? (feminine, vowel start)

16. Which article goes before Russie? (feminine, consonant start)

17. Which article goes before Colombie? (feminine, consonant start)

18. Which article goes before Tunisie? (feminine, consonant start)

19. Which article goes before Sénégal? (masculine, consonant start)

20. Which article goes before Maroc? (masculine, consonant start)

21. Which article goes before Portugal? (masculine, consonant start)

22. Which article goes before Inde? (feminine, vowel start)

23. Which article goes before Australie? (feminine, vowel start)

24. Which article goes before Égypte? (feminine, vowel start)

25. Which article goes before Suisse? (feminine, consonant start)

26. Which article goes before Belgique? (feminine, consonant start)

27. Which article goes before Turquie? (feminine, consonant start)

28. Which article goes before Indonésie? (feminine, vowel start)

29. Which article goes before Israël? (masculine, vowel start)

30. Which article goes before Irak? (masculine, vowel start)

31. Which article goes before Philippines? (plural)

32. Which article goes before Émirats arabes unis? (plural)

33. Which article goes before Cuba? (masculine island)

34. Which article goes before Thaïlande? (feminine, consonant start)

35. Which article goes before Madagascar? (feminine island)

36. Which article goes before Haïti? (masculine, silent H = vowel)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Nationalities

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Derive the nationality adjective from the country and agree it with the speaker: français → française, chinois → chinoise, américain → américaine. Two rules drilled together: the m/f ending pattern and spelling. Observe differences in capitalisation among the options.

1. Ce sont des ___ . They are French people.

2. Marie est ___ . Marie is French.

3. Pierre et Marie sont ___ . Pierre and Marie are French.

4. Marie et Léa sont ___ . Marie and Léa are French.

5. Emma est ___ . Emma is English.

6. Sofia est ___ . Sofia is Italian.

7. Sophie est ___ . Sophie is Belgian.

8. Wei est ___ . Wei is Singaporean.

9. Jun est ___ . Jun is Singaporean.

10. Sur le formulaire, dans le champ « nationalité » : ___ . On the form, under the field "nationality": ___.

11. Diego est ___ . Diego is Spanish.

12. Anna, Lukas et Felix sont ___ . Anna, Lukas, and Felix are German.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Negation

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Wrap the conjugated verb in ne … pas. Drills the two-piece structure and the elision ne → n' before a vowel — je ne suis pas but je n'ai pas, je n'aime pas.

1. Correcting an assumption Non, je ___ français. Je suis singapourien. No, I'm not French. I'm Singaporean.

2. Correcting a guess Non, elle ___ anglaise. Elle est française. No, she isn't English. She's French.

3. A friend is wrong about your major Non, tu ___ étudiant en physique. Je suis étudiant en français. No, you're not a physics student. I'm a French student.

4. Pointing out a misidentification Non, il ___ Pierre. Il est Marc. No, he's not Pierre. He's Marc.

5. Group identity correction Non, nous ___ français. Nous sommes belges. No, we're not French. We're Belgian.

6. Polite correction Non, je ___ Madame Tan. Je suis Madame Lim. No, I'm not Mrs Tan. I'm Mrs Lim.

7. Correcting your profession Non, je ___ professeur. Je suis étudiant. No, I'm not a teacher. I'm a student.

8. Language clarification Non, je ___ allemand. Je parle anglais et français. No, I don't speak German. I speak English and French.

9. Where you live Non, je ___ à Paris. J'habite à Singapour. No, I don't live in Paris. I live in Singapore.

10. Correcting your age Non, je ___ 20 ans. J'ai 22 ans. No, I'm not 20. I'm 22.

11. Misidentified group Non, ils ___ étudiants. Ils sont touristes. No, they're not students. They're tourists.

12. Casual conversation (informal register) Non, je ___ fatigué. Nah, I'm not tired. (casual)

13. Wei ___ française. Wei is not French.

14. Nous ___ en retard. We are not late.

15. Yuki ___ le café. Yuki does not like coffee.

16. Mateo ___ japonais. Mateo does not speak Japanese.

17. Vous ___ américaine, Madame Sow ? You are not American, Madame Sow?

18. Amara ___ le samedi. Amara does not work on Saturdays.

19. Je ___ à Marseille. I do not live in Marseille.

20. Les croissants ___ gratuits. The croissants are not free.

21. Je ___ de stylo. I don't have a pen.

22. Tu ___ à la cantine ? You don't eat at the cafeteria?

23. Ce ___ un problème. It is not a problem.

24. Je ___ la question. I don't understand the question.

25. Les étudiants ___ de livres. The students don't have books.

26. Je ___ partir maintenant. I don't want to leave now.

27. Mateo ___ le soir. Mateo does not study in the evening.

28. Vous ___ la réponse ? You don't know the answer? (teacher to class)

29. Tu ___ le froid, Wei ? You don't like the cold, Wei?

30. La boulangerie ___ le dimanche. The bakery does not close on Sundays.

31. Les étudiants ___ en classe. The students are not listening in class.

32. Je ___ d'accord avec toi. I don't agree with you.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Épisodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4

Unit 1Negation — word order

Focus — Place ne/n' and pas correctly around the verb. Tap tiles to build each sentence.

1. Put in order: I am not French.

2. Put in order: She is not English.

3. Put in order: You are not a student.

4. Put in order: We are not Japanese.

5. Put in order: They are not Spanish.

6. Put in order: I am not twenty years old.

7. Put in order: He does not speak French.

8. Put in order: You are not a teacher.

9. Put in order: We don't eat here.

10. Put in order: She doesn't like Paris.

11. Put in order: I don't understand.

12. Put in order: You don't want to eat.

13. Put in order: We don't have a cat.

14. Put in order: They don't go to the cinema.

15. Put in order: I am not tired.

16. Put in order: He doesn't live in Paris.

LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Avoir and age

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Conjugate avoir and use it for age — French says J'ai 20 ans, literally "I have 20 years". Two traps drilled together: the irregular avoir paradigm (ai, as, a, avons, avez, ont) and the verb swap from English be to French avoir.

1. Stating your own age J' ___ 20 ans. I'm 20 years old.

2. Reporting someone else's age Pierre ___ 25 ans. Pierre is 25.

3. Asking a peer their age Tu ___ quel âge ? How old are you?

4. Stating an age Marie a ___ ans. Marie is 21.

5. Stating an age Léa a ___ ans. Léa is 17.

6. Stating an age Tom a ___ ans. Tom is 14.

7. Group age statement Mes amis ___ 22 ans. My friends are 22.

8. Stating an age Marc a ___ ans. Marc is 35.

9. Stating "we" age Nous ___ 19 ans. We are 19.

10. Specifying a baby's age Le bébé a un ___ . The baby is one year old.

11. Asking grandparent's age Ma grand-mère a ___ ans. My grandmother is 41.

12. Stating an age Mon père a ___ ans. My father is 60.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Avoir and physical states

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Use avoir for physical sensations and idiomatic states: avoir faim, soif, chaud, froid, peur, sommeil, raison, mal à…. French treats these as things you have, not what you are. Never Je suis faim.

1. Madame Benali demande à Wei : « Vous avez ___ ? »
(It's noon, lunchtime.)

2. Yuki en classe en hiver à Paris : « J'ai ___ ! Ferme la fenêtre, s'il te plaît. »

3. Mateo après une longue journée : « Je ___ sommeil. Je rentre. »

4. Amara devant un grand chien : « J'ai ___ du chien. »

5. Wei boit beaucoup d'eau : « J'ai ___ aujourd'hui. »

6. Mateo en été à Paris : « Il fait 32°C. J'ai ___ ! »

7. Quel âge as-tu, Yuki ? — « J'___ 21 ans. »

8. La prof dit à Mateo : « Tu ___ raison ! Bravo. »

9. Madame Benali à 7h du matin : « Vous avez ___ ? Asseyez-vous, je fais un café. »

10. Wei en hiver à La Fougère : « Amara et Yuki ___ très froid. »
(Two people = plural subject.)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Focus
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsQuestions — tilesIdentity capstone

Unit 1Avoir vs être

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Choose avoir or être for each state. The test: is the word a noun (faim, soif, peur, mal) → avoir; or an adjective that changes form for masc/fem (fatigué/fatiguée, content/contente, triste, malade) → être. This is the most common verb-choice mistake in French.

1. Madame Benali à 7h du matin : « Wei, tu ___ fatigué ! Assieds-toi. »

2. Mateo n'a rien mangé depuis ce matin : « J'___ très faim ! »

3. Yuki a de la fièvre : « Je ___ malade. Je rentre. »

4. Amara devant un examen : « J'___ peur de rater ! »

5. Wei à la fin de la journée : « Je ___ content de mon travail. »

6. Mateo au gymnase après le sport : « J'___ très chaud ! »

7. À l'enterrement : « Madame Benali ___ triste aujourd'hui. »

8. Wei en hiver : « Mateo et moi, nous ___ froid. »

9. Yuki au déjeuner : « Je ___ 19 ans. »

10. Amara appelle son médecin : « Bonjour, je ___ malade. J'___ mal à la tête. »
Pick the pair.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Focus
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsIdentity capstone

Unit 1Languages and questions

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — Combine language vocabulary (je parle français, anglais, mandarin) with question forms — Tu parles … ? Quelles langues parlez-vous ? Tests no capital on language names and basic question framing for languages.

1. Asking about a language preference « ___ langue tu veux apprendre ? » Which language do you want to learn?

2. Asking about multiple languages « ___ langues vous parlez ? » Which languages do you speak?

3. Asking about origin « Tu viens de ___ pays ? » Which country are you from?

4. Asking about multiple destinations « ___ pays tu visites ? » Which countries are you visiting?

5. Stating your own languages Je ___ français et anglais. I speak French and English.

6. Reporting what others speak Mes parents ___ chinois et anglais. My parents speak Chinese and English.

7. Talking about your family À la maison, nous ___ français. At home, we speak French.

8. Avoiding the English-influenced article Je parle ___ . I speak French.

9. Filling out a form: language listed as adjective Marie parle ___ . Marie speaks French.

10. Listing two languages À Singapour, on parle anglais ___ chinois. In Singapore, people speak English and Chinese.

11. Correcting an assumption Non, je ___ allemand. No, I don't speak German.

12. Forming a complete question ___ Marc parle-t-il ? (il en parle plusieurs) What languages does Marc speak? (he speaks several)

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group
Keep identity language clear
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.

Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Épisodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4

Unit 1Questions — word order

Focus — Form questions using est-ce que, inversion, or question words. Tap tiles to build each question.

1. Put in order: Do you speak French?

2. Put in order: What is your name? (formal)

3. Put in order: Where is the train station?

4. Put in order: What time is it?

5. Put in order: Are you French?

6. Put in order: How are you? (formal)

7. Put in order: Where do you live?

8. Put in order: How old are you?

9. Put in order: Does he speak English?

10. Put in order: Why are you late?

11. Put in order: When do you arrive?

12. Put in order: How many languages do you speak?

13. Put in order: Where are you from?

14. Put in order: What are you doing?

15. Put in order: What is his name?

16. Put in order: Do you like Paris?

LAF1201

Unit 1 · Identity and introductions

Episodes 1–2 bridge — identity in the new group

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
IntroductionsIntroductions — tilesSubject pronoun framesÊtre and identityÊtre/avoir — tilesProfessionsCountriesNationalitiesNegationNegation — tilesAvoir and ageAvoir and physical statesAvoir vs êtreLanguages and questionsIdentity capstone

Unit 1Identity capstone

The group introduces itself in class and begins to understand the Francophone world around the boulangerie and La Fougère.
Keep identity language clear: être for identity, avoir for age, lowercase nationality adjectives and language names where appropriate.
Focus — One evening, one event: International night. You arrive and greet your professor, switch register to chat with peers at the café, leave with thanks, ask the receptionist for a form, meet the professor's daughter, fill in your nationality, get asked your age, correct a wrong assumption, ask new friends what they speak, and close by introducing someone else to the room. The arc is arrival → mingling → form-filling → introducing others — and along the way every Unit 1 system has to fire in the moment the scene calls for it.

1. First day of class — meeting your French professor You say: ___ Hello madame, I am Wei.

2. At the campus café — peers your age You say: ___ Hi! I'm Wei.

3. Leaving the professor's office after a meeting You say: ___ Goodbye madame, thank you very much.

4. Asking the receptionist for a form You ask: ___ Hello madame, may I have the form, please?

5. Meeting your professor's 10-year-old daughter at a faculty event You ask the child: ___ Hi Léa! How old are you?

6. Form-filling: stating your nationality (Amara is writing) You write: ___ I am Singaporean.

7. Asked your age in a casual meet-up You answer: ___ I'm 21.

8. Correcting a wrong assumption about you You reply: ___ No, I'm not Chinese. I'm Singaporean.

9. Asking new friends what they speak You ask: ___ What languages do you speak?

10. Spotting where French liaison happens In which phrase does liaison happen? ___ You are French.

11. The orthographic-phonological gap Which sentence is correctly written? ___ My friends speak French.

12. A full third-person introduction You say: ___ Madame, this is Lina. She's Singaporean, 22 years old, and a student.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Keep identity language clear

LAF1201

Unit 2 · Daily life above the boulangerie

Épisode 2 — Une famille au-dessus de la boulangerie

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4

Everyday routines, places, objects, time, notes, and messages — life in the apartment above Madame Benali’s boulangerie.

1Faire2Aller3Vouloir4C’est / ce sont and negation5Articles and countable objects6Origin and source: de + le = du7Preferences8Time9Objects and description10Places and activities11Phrases in context★Daily-life capstone13Possessives
LAF1201

Unit 2 · Daily life above the boulangerie

Épisode 2 — Une famille au-dessus de la boulangerie

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
FaireAller àVouloirC’est / ce sont and negationArticles and countable objectsOrigin and source: de + le = duPreferencesTimeObjects and descriptionPlaces and activitiesPhrases in contextDaily-life capstonePossessives

Unit 2Faire

Daily life moves between the apartment, the boulangerie, La Fougère, the market, and the classroom.
Daily-life French: faire (to do/make), aller (to go), vouloir (to want), articles le/la/les, telling time, places and activities, and possessives (mon, ton, son…).
Focus — Conjugate faire across all six persons and use it in the high-frequency activity expressions (faire du sport, faire les courses, faire la cuisine). The verb is irregular — faisons, faites, font are the three forms most likely to slip.

1. Stating your own activity Le weekend, je ___ du sport. On weekends, I work out.

2. Asking a friend Tu ___ quoi ce soir ? What are you doing tonight?

3. Reporting what someone else does Pierre ___ de la natation. Pierre swims.

4. Group activity (we) Nous ___ des promenades le dimanche. We go for walks on Sundays.

5. Asking a group politely Vous ___ de la musique ? Do you play music?

6. Group activity (they) Mes amis ___ du foot le samedi. My friends play football on Saturdays.

7. Faire vs jouer (sport) Le mardi soir, je ___ du sport au gymnase. On Tuesday evenings, I work out at the gym.

8. Faire + activity (cycling) Avec mes amis, je ___ du vélo. With my friends, I go cycling.

9. Faire + cultural activity Léa ___ de la musique. Léa does music / plays music.

10. Asking what someone does for a living/hobby Qu'est-ce que vous ___ comme sport ? What sport do you do?

11. Negative form of faire Non, je ___ pas de sport. No, I don't do any sport.

12. Idiomatic faire (weather) Aujourd'hui, il ___ beau. It's nice weather today.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 2 — Une famille au-dessus de la boulangerie
Focus
Daily-life French: faire (to do/make), aller (to go), vouloir (to want), articles le/la/les, telling time, places and activities, and possessives (mon, ton, son…).
Focus
One correct answer per question unless a page explicitly says otherwise.
Unit 2 note
Unit 2 note: du is a contraction such as de + le, not a partitive article.

Reader alignment

Characters: Wei, Amara, Mateo, Yuki, Madame Benali, Madame Sow.
Settings: boulangerie, apartment above it, classroom, La Fougère, Paris streets, market, dinner, and Bilan.
LAF1201

Unit 2 · Daily life above the boulangerie

Épisode 2 — Une famille au-dessus de la boulangerie

PratiqueU0U1U2U3U4
FaireAller àVouloirC’est / ce sont and negationArticles and countable objectsOrigin and source: de + le = duPreferencesTimeObjects and descriptionPlaces and activitiesPhrases in contextDaily-life capstonePossessives

Unit 2Aller à

Daily life moves between the apartment, the boulangerie, La Fougère, the market, and the classroom.
Daily-life French: faire (to do/make), aller (to go), vouloir (to want), articles le/la/les, telling time, places and activities, and possessives (mon, ton, son…).
Focus — Conjugate aller and pair it with destinations. Two systems drilled together: the irregular paradigm (vais, vas, va, allons, allez, vont) and the à + article contractions that follow it — au cinéma, à la boulangerie, aux États-Unis.

1. Stating where you're going Je ___ au cinéma ce soir. I'm going to the cinema tonight.

2. Asking a friend Tu ___ où ? Where are you going?

3. Reporting a friend's plan Marc ___ à la piscine. Marc is going to the pool.

4. Group plan Nous ___ au restaurant samedi. We're going to the restaurant on Saturday.

5. Asking a group Vous ___ à Paris cet été ? Are you going to Paris this summer?

6. Group destination (they) Mes amis ___ au concert ce soir. My friends are going to the concert tonight.

7. Contraction: à + le Je vais ___ cinéma. I'm going to the cinema.

8. Contraction: à + la (no contraction) Léa va ___ piscine le mardi. Léa goes to the pool on Tuesdays.

9. Contraction: à + les Je vais ___ toilettes, je reviens. I'm going to the toilet, I'll be back.

10. Contraction: à + l' (vowel) Le patient va ___ hôpital. The patient is going to the hospital.

11. Cities take bare à L'année prochaine, je vais ___ Paris. Next year, I'm going to Paris.

12. Negation with aller Non, je ___ au gymnase aujourd'hui. No, I'm not going to the gym today.

Answer the questions, then check your answers.

Grammar card

Focus
Épisode 2 — Une famille au-dessus de la boulangerie
Focus
